title: Wal-Mart Wars: Moral Populism in the Twenty-First Century By Rebekah Peeples Massengill New York University Press. 2013. 256 pages. $24 (paper)
abstract: Does Walmart represent the best or worst of capitalism? Do the low prices made possible by its global supply chain and outsized economic leverage make it an indispensable benefactor for American consumers? Or do its low wages and exploitative labor practices make it the arch-nemesis of the American worker? In either case, for both supporters and critics, Walmart (until 2008, Wal-Mart) has become the apotheosis of modern global capitalism, making it a flashpoint in public discourse and a revealing focus of scholarly inquiry. In her insightful and finely wrought study, Rebekah Peeples Massengill uses public debate over the virtues and failings of Walmart to examine the moral tensions inherent in modern capitalism. Her analysis offers insights concerning the moral underpinnings of economic discourse, the diffusion of group claims in the public sphere, the rhetorical centrality of the American family in conservative populism, and the shortcomings of progressive advocacy. Massengill's jumping-off point is that in order to make effective claims on the American imagination, economic discourse must also be moral discourse. Among other things, such discourse defines identities, establishes standards of evaluation, and creates a sense of imagined community. Concretely, Massengill focuses on the discourse produced by three “K Street” organizations—Wal-Mart Watch (backed by the Service Employees International Union), Working Families for Wal-Mart (backed by Wal-Mart, Inc.), and Wal-Mart, Inc., itself—during 2005–2006. This was a period of notable controversy over allegations of gender discrimination and workplace exploitation, and revelations that nearly half of the children of Walmart's hourly employees were either uninsured or eligible for Medicaid. Beyond examining nearly 1,000 documents produced by the three advocacy organizations, she also examines newspaper coverage of Walmart from 2000 to 2006, which allowed her to trace the changing tenor of public debate as advocates' claims got picked up by major national news outlets in the latter years of this period.
